howTo.name: How to convert JPG and PNG images to WebP
howTo.description: How to convert JPG and PNG images to WebP format. Quality settings (60–95%), Smart Quality mechanism, and downloading converted files as ZIP.

steps.name: Add files for conversion
steps.text: Drag JPG or PNG images onto the designated area or select files from your device. You can add multiple files at once.

steps.name: Set quality level
steps.text: Choose the output WebP file quality using the slider (60–95%). The default value of 80% is a good balance between quality and size.

steps.name: Start conversion
steps.text: After starting conversion, the tool will process all files in the queue and automatically apply Smart Quality.

steps.name: Download converted files
steps.text: Converted files can be downloaded individually or all at once as a ZIP archive.

contentBlocks.title: What is WebP and why convert images?
contentBlocks.html:
WebP is an image format created by Google that allows reducing image file sizes by up to 25–35% compared to JPG and PNG - while maintaining comparable visual quality. Smaller files mean faster page loading, which translates to better user experience and higher scores in PageSpeed Insights.

The WebP format is supported by all modern browsers: Chrome, Firefox, Safari (from version 14), and Edge. If you run a website, online store, or blog, converting images to WebP is one of the simplest ways to improve site performance.

The converter supports both JPG photos and PNG graphics (including those with transparency). You can convert any number of files at once - all are processed locally in the browser, without being sent to a server.

contentBlocks.title: How to convert images to WebP
contentBlocks.description: Converting to WebP takes just a few seconds:

items.title: 1. Add files
items.description: Drag JPG or PNG images onto the designated area or select files from your device. You can add any number of files at once.

items.title: 2. Set quality
items.description: Choose the quality level (default 80%). A lower value means a smaller file. For most use cases, 80% is the optimal setting.

items.title: 3. Download WebP
items.description: After conversion, download files individually or as a ZIP archive. Each file shows information about the space saved.

contentBlocks.title: How to add files for conversion
contentBlocks.html:
The tool offers two ways to add files:

  • Drag and drop - grab files from a folder on your computer and drop them onto the area labeled "Drag and drop images here". You can drag multiple files at once.
  • Select from device - clicking the file upload area opens a selection dialog. Holding Ctrl (or Cmd on Mac) lets you select multiple files at once.

The tool only accepts JPG and PNG files. If you accidentally add a file in another format (e.g., GIF or BMP), it will be automatically skipped and you will see an informational message.

Privacy: All files are processed locally in the browser. They are not sent anywhere - they do not go to any server. After closing the tab or browser, files are removed from memory.

contentBlocks.title: What do file statuses mean?
contentBlocks.html:
Each file in the queue has one of four statuses that indicate conversion progress:

Pending

The file is in the queue and waiting to be processed. Conversion has not yet started.

Processing…

The file is being converted. For most images this takes a fraction of a second, but very large files may take several seconds.

Done

Conversion completed successfully. The WebP file is ready to download. Next to the status you will also see the size before and after conversion.

Error

Something went wrong during conversion. This may be a corrupted source file or a browser memory issue with very large images. The "Retry" button lets you try again.

contentBlocks.title: How does Smart Quality work?
contentBlocks.html:
Smart Quality is an automatic optimization mechanism that ensures the output WebP file is never larger than the original. It works as follows:

  1. The tool converts the image at your set quality (e.g., 80%).
  2. It checks whether the resulting file is smaller than the original.
  3. If it is larger, it automatically lowers quality and tries again.
  4. The process repeats until the output file is smaller or quality drops below a safe minimum.

The tool automatically selects optimal settings. If you have an image that is already heavily compressed (e.g., a JPG at 60% quality), the parameters will be automatically adjusted to still achieve size savings.

Next to each file you will see "WebP quality used" - this is the actual quality applied after any Smart Quality adjustment.

contentBlocks.title: Quality settings - what to choose?
contentBlocks.demoHtml:

contentBlocks.html:
The quality slider lets you set a value from 60% to 95%. A higher value means better image quality but also a larger file size.

  • 80% (default) - a good balance for most use cases.
  • 85–90% - for product photos and photography portfolios.
  • 60–70% - when minimizing file size is the priority.

contentBlocks.demoHtml:

contentBlocks.html:
After conversion is complete, you have several download options:

  • Download (next to each file) - downloads a single WebP file.
  • Download all - downloads all files one by one.
  • Download as ZIP - creates an archive with all files.

CSV report option: Include a conversion summary report in the ZIP archive.

contentBlocks.title: Savings report - what does it show?
contentBlocks.html:
Below the conversion buttons you will find a summary with savings information:

  • Total input size - sum of all original file sizes.
  • Total size after conversion - sum of all WebP file sizes.
  • Saved - how much space you gained through conversion (in KB/MB and percentage).

Copy summary: The button copies the report to clipboard in text format. You can paste it into notes, emails, or documents - useful for documenting image optimization.

contentBlocks.title: How much can you save by converting to WebP?
contentBlocks.html:
Savings depend on the type of image and its original compression. Below are example results for typical files:

JPG photo (camera)

2.4 MB → 890 KB

Savings: ~63%

PNG graphic (logo)

180 KB → 45 KB

Savings: ~75%

Product photo

500 KB → 185 KB

Savings: ~63%

Actual savings may vary. The converter shows the exact size before and after conversion for each file, as well as a summary of total savings.

contentBlocks.title: What makes this JPG/PNG to WebP converter special?

items.title: Full privacy
items.description: Files never leave your computer. Conversion happens in the browser - nothing is sent to a server. After closing the page, data is removed.

items.title: Batch conversion of multiple files
items.description: Convert any number of images at once. All files are processed sequentially, and results are available for download individually or as a ZIP archive.

items.title: Smart Quality
items.description: The tool automatically adjusts quality so the output file is always smaller than the original. No need to manually search for optimal settings.

items.title: Download as ZIP
items.description: Download converted files individually or all at once as a ZIP archive. Optionally, you can include a CSV report with a summary.

contentBlocks.title: Things to keep in mind
contentBlocks.description: A few tips to help avoid problems during conversion:

items.title: Very large files
items.description: Converting images above 4000×4000 pixels may be slower and strain the browser. If processing many large files, consider splitting them into batches.

items.title: Already compressed images
items.description: If the original JPG was saved at very low quality, converting to WebP may not yield large savings. In extreme cases, the WebP file may be even larger - Smart Quality will then lower quality automatically.

items.title: PNG format with transparency
items.description: WebP supports transparency, so PNG files with an alpha channel will be converted correctly. However, size savings for PNGs with transparency may be less than for regular JPG photos.

items.title: Browser compatibility
items.description: All modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari 14+, Edge) support WebP. If the site must work on older browsers, you can use the <picture> tag with an alternative JPG/PNG source.

contentBlocks.title: When is it worth converting images to WebP?
contentBlocks.description: Converting to WebP brings benefits in virtually every case when images are displayed in a browser:

items.title: Business websites
items.description: Faster loading improves first impressions and reduces bounce rate.

items.title: Online stores
items.description: Product photos are often the largest files on a page; optimizing them speeds up the entire store.

items.title: Blogs and portals
items.description: Articles with many photos load significantly faster.

items.title: Photography portfolios
items.description: At 85–90% quality, the visual difference is unnoticeable, and files are smaller.

items.title: Web apps (PWA)
items.description: Smaller assets mean faster performance, especially on mobile devices.

contentBlocks.title: WebP, AVIF, and JPEG XL — modern image formats compared
contentBlocks.html:
Three next-generation image formats compete for adoption on the modern web: WebP, AVIF, and JPEG XL. Each offers better compression than traditional JPG and PNG, but they differ in browser support, encoding speed, and feature set.

WebP was developed by Google and released in 2010. It supports both lossy and lossless compression, transparency (alpha channel), and animation. As of early 2025, WebP has over 97% global browser support according to caniuse.com — Chrome, Firefox, Safari (since version 14), Edge, and Opera all render WebP natively. This broad compatibility makes WebP the safest choice for production websites today.

AVIF is based on the AV1 video codec and offers even better compression ratios than WebP — files can be 20–30% smaller at equivalent visual quality. Chrome, Firefox, and Samsung Internet support AVIF. Safari added AVIF support in version 16.4 (March 2023). The main drawback is encoding speed: AVIF compression is significantly slower than WebP, which makes batch processing of large image libraries more time-consuming.

JPEG XL was designed as a universal replacement for legacy JPEG. It supports lossless transcoding from existing JPG files (reversible, no quality loss), progressive decoding, and HDR content. However, Chrome removed JPEG XL support in version 110 (February 2023), and Firefox has not enabled it by default. Without major browser support, JPEG XL remains impractical for web delivery in 2025.

For most websites, WebP delivers the best balance of compression, compatibility, and encoding speed. A forward-looking approach uses the HTML <picture> element to serve AVIF to browsers that support it, with WebP as the fallback and JPG/PNG as the final fallback for legacy browsers.

contentBlocks.title: Image optimization and Core Web Vitals
contentBlocks.html:
Core Web Vitals are a set of performance metrics that Google uses as ranking signals. One of them — LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) — measures the time until the largest visible element renders on screen. On many pages, this element is an image: a hero banner, a product photo, or a featured graphic. Uncompressed JPG and PNG files weighing 1–3 MB delay LCP and lower PageSpeed Insights scores.

Converting images to WebP reduces file sizes by 25–35% while maintaining comparable visual quality. Smaller image files shorten resource download time, which directly improves the LCP metric. Additional techniques — such as the loading="lazy" attribute for below-the-fold images, the <picture> element with srcset for serving appropriate sizes, and fetchpriority="high" for the hero image — collectively accelerate page rendering.

The second metric — CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) — tracks layout shifts during page load. Images without declared dimensions (width and height attributes) cause content jumps after the image loads. Declaring dimensions in HTML or CSS eliminates this issue regardless of the image format.

Responsive image delivery through the srcset attribute allows the browser to choose the optimal file size based on viewport width and device pixel ratio. A mobile visitor on a 375px-wide screen does not need a 2400px-wide hero image. Serving a 750px version in WebP can reduce file size by 80% compared to the full-resolution JPG original.

Tools like PageSpeed Insights, Lighthouse, and WebPageTest display detailed data about image impact on performance. The Lighthouse report in the Performance category identifies specific files worth converting to a next-generation format.

contentBlocks.title: Product photo optimization for e-commerce
contentBlocks.html:
Product images account for the majority of page weight in online stores. A category page with 40 thumbnails and a product page with a gallery of 5–8 photos can load tens of megabytes of images on each visit. Converting these graphics to WebP reduces data transfer and speeds up loading — particularly on mobile devices, where over 60% of global e-commerce traffic originates.

Category page thumbnails should be sized to match the display grid — a 200×200 px file weighing 8–15 KB in WebP loads faster than a downscaled 1200×1200 px original. Product zoom images can retain higher quality (85–90%) because they load only after user interaction (click or hover).

Hero images above the fold on the homepage and landing pages deserve special attention. These images should be served with fetchpriority="high" and preloaded via a <link rel="preload"> tag. Converting the hero image from JPG to WebP at 80% quality typically saves 30–50% of file weight without perceptible quality loss at normal viewing distance.

Page load speed directly affects conversion rates in e-commerce. Industry research consistently shows that additional loading time increases the probability of a visitor abandoning the page. Optimizing product images is one of the simplest actions that reduce load time without requiring code changes to the store platform.

contentBlocks.title: Image SEO: file names, alt text, and Google Images
contentBlocks.html:
Search engines cannot interpret image content directly — they rely on surrounding signals to understand what an image depicts. Three primary signals drive image SEO: the file name, the alt attribute, and structured data.

Descriptive file names help search engines categorize images before indexing. A file named "red-leather-handbag-front-view.webp" provides more context than "IMG_4523.webp". Use hyphens to separate words in file names. Avoid underscores, spaces, or encoded characters. The file name should describe the image content concisely and include the primary keyword when natural.

The alt attribute serves two purposes: accessibility (screen readers announce it to visually impaired users) and SEO (search engines use it as a content signal). Write alt text that describes the image in a short phrase — "woman wearing a red leather handbag on a city street" is more useful than "handbag" or "product image 1". Avoid keyword stuffing in alt attributes — search engines detect this and may devalue the page.

An image sitemap helps Google discover images that might not be found through regular crawling — for example, images loaded via JavaScript or lazy loading. The sitemap lists image URLs alongside their parent page URLs. Google Search Console shows which images from the sitemap have been indexed.

ImageObject structured data (schema.org) provides additional metadata: caption, author, license, and content URL. Product pages benefit from structured data that links the product image to the product schema. Google Images and Google Lens use these signals when ranking visual search results.

contentBlocks.title: CMS image handling: WordPress, Shopify, and static sites
contentBlocks.html:
WordPress — the most widely used CMS globally — supports native WebP uploads since version 5.8. Plugins such as Imagify, ShortPixel, and EWWW Image Optimizer enable automatic conversion of images during upload to the media library. Each plugin generates a WebP variant alongside the original and serves it to browsers that support the format. Configuration typically takes a few minutes and applies to all future uploads.

Shopify automatically converts product images to WebP when serving them through its CDN. Store owners do not need to install any additional apps for basic WebP delivery. However, images in custom theme sections, blog posts, and static pages may bypass automatic conversion. For these cases, manually converting images before upload ensures consistent optimization across the entire store.

WooCommerce stores on self-hosted WordPress benefit from the same plugin ecosystem. ShortPixel and Imagify both integrate with WooCommerce product galleries, converting product images and generating responsive srcset variants. Bulk optimization features process existing media libraries without re-uploading files.

Static site generators and modern frameworks — Next.js, Astro, Gatsby — offer built-in image optimization components. The Next.js Image component automatically generates WebP and AVIF variants, applies responsive sizing, and lazy-loads images below the fold. Astro and Gatsby provide similar functionality through their image processing pipelines.

CDN services such as Cloudflare (Polish feature), BunnyCDN, and Fastly offer server-side image conversion to WebP. Images are transformed on the edge server without changes to the origin. This approach works well for sites with large image libraries where manual conversion would be impractical.

contentBlocks.title: Frequently asked questions about the WebP converter

items.question: What is WebP and how does it differ from JPG?
items.answer: WebP is an image format created by Google that combines the advantages of JPG (small photo files) and PNG (transparency). WebP files are on average 25–35% smaller than JPG at comparable visual quality. All modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) support WebP.
items.answerSchemaText: WebP is a Google image format, 25–35% smaller than JPG. All modern browsers support it.

items.question: Does WebP support transparency like PNG?
items.answer: Yes. WebP supports the alpha channel (transparency) - PNG files with transparent backgrounds will be converted correctly. However, size savings for PNGs with transparency may be less than for regular JPG photos.
items.answerSchemaText: Yes, WebP supports alpha transparency. PNG files with transparent backgrounds convert correctly.

items.question: What quality level should I choose?
items.answer: For most use cases (websites, stores, blogs), 80% quality provides a good balance between file size and appearance. For product photos or portfolios, you can choose 85–90%. Values below 70% will significantly reduce size but may introduce visible artifacts.
items.answerSchemaText: 80% for most uses. 85–90% for product photos. Below 70% for maximum compression.

items.question: How much space can I save?
items.answer: Typical savings are 25–35% compared to JPG. For example, a 500 KB JPG photo after conversion to WebP will be about 325–375 KB. For PNG, savings can be even greater - up to 50–70%.
items.answerSchemaText: 25–35% for JPG, up to 50–70% for PNG.

items.question: Can I convert multiple files at once?
items.answer: Yes. You can add any number of files at once - there is no limit. All will be converted, and you can download them individually or as a ZIP archive.
items.answerSchemaText: Yes, unlimited batch conversion with ZIP download.

items.question: What is Smart Quality?
items.answer: Smart Quality is an automatic quality adjustment mechanism that ensures the output WebP file is never larger than the original. If conversion would result in a larger file (which can happen with already heavily compressed images), the tool automatically lowers quality until the output is smaller.
items.answerSchemaText: Automatic quality adjustment ensuring the output file is always smaller than the original.

items.question: Does WebP speed up a website?
items.answer: Yes. Smaller image files mean faster page loading. This directly impacts the LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) metric in Core Web Vitals - one of Google's ranking factors. Tools like PageSpeed Insights often recommend converting images to WebP.
items.answerSchemaText: Yes, smaller files improve LCP and Core Web Vitals scores.
